message:
Add severity field to explain output and trace info

- Add severity to PatternMatch in evaluator.rs with ast_severity_to_pack_severity converter
- Update denied_by_pack_pattern helpers to accept severity parameter
- Add severity field to MatchInfo creation in cli.rs
- Update all test fixtures in trace.rs with appropriate severity values
- Regression tests for severity stability already present in packs/mod.rs

Part of git_safety_guard-1gt.3.2: Tag existing rules with severity.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -12,15 +12,15 @@
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.2.4","title":"Allowlist: conditions, expiration, and wildcard safety rules","description":"## Objective

Implement the more advanced allowlist semantics in a way that is safe and predictable.

## Semantics to support

### 1) Expiration

- `expires_at` field on entries.
- Expired entries:
  - evaluator: ignored
  - validate: warning (or error if `--strict`)

### 2) Conditions (environment gating)

- `conditions = { KEY = "VAL" }` maps to environment variables.
- Conditions are ANDed.
- Missing env var means condition not met.

### 3) Wildcards

- Allow `pack_id:*` (scoped to one pack).
- Disallow `*:*`.
- Allow `pack_id:prefix-*` only if we commit to stable naming conventions.

### 4) Risk acknowledgement

- Regex allowlist entries require `risk_acknowledged=true`.
- Validation should warn on overly broad regex (heuristics).

## Acceptance criteria

- Expiration works and is test-covered.
- Conditions work (CI-only allowlists etc).
- Wildcard constraints prevent unsafe global bypass.

## Tests

- Expiration tests using fixed timestamps.
- Condition tests with env var manipulation.
- Wildcard constraint tests.
","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-07T21:05:14.997598561-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-08T13:31:36.510245959-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-08T13:31:36.510245959-05:00","close_reason":"Completed: implemented is_expired, conditions_met, has_required_risk_ack, is_entry_valid with 16 tests","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.2.4","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.2","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-07T21:05:24.524137941-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.2.4","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.2.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-07T21:22:01.654562185-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.2.4","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.2.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-07T21:22:06.749684456-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.2.5","title":"Allowlist: comprehensive tests + documentation","description":"## Objective

Make allowlisting safe in practice by shipping:

- comprehensive automated tests
- clear user documentation

## Tests

### Parser/validation

- invalid TOML does not crash hook
- missing required fields surfaced in validate
- risky entries require acknowledgement

### Evaluator integration

- allowlist overrides only the intended rule
- allowlisting does not disable other packs
- allowlist layer precedence works

### CLI

- add/list/remove idempotence
- validate output contains actionable messages

## Docs

- README section: "Resolving false positives" describing:
  - `dcg explain`
  - `dcg allowlist add ...`
  - recommended allowlist shapes
  - security caveats

## Acceptance criteria

- Tests cover the core allowlist behaviors and guard against regressions.
- Docs are sufficient that a user can resolve a false positive without reading source.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.2.6","title":"Allowlist: E2E coverage in scripts/e2e_test.sh (hook path)","description":"## Objective

Add end-to-end coverage proving allowlists affect the **real hook path** (stdin JSON → decision) and do not create accidental broad bypasses.

## Why E2E is required

Allowlisting is a high-risk feature:

- It must override only the intended rule.
- It must work in hook mode (not just `dcg test`).
- It must not silently disable packs or normalization.

## Test cases (must include)

1) Baseline: a known catastrophic command is blocked
- e.g., `git reset --hard` (record the rule_id used)

2) Project allowlist overrides that exact rule
- Create `.dcg/allowlist.toml` in an isolated fixture repo/cwd.
- Re-run the same hook JSON input.
- Expected: ALLOW (or WARN if configured) with **no JSON deny**.

3) Non-target rule remains enforced
- Ensure another blocked command is still blocked (e.g., `git clean -fd`).

4) Expired allowlist entry does not apply

5) Unsafe wildcard entry is rejected (or requires risk_ack)

## Logging requirements

E2E output should print:

- the rule_id being allowlisted
- which allowlist layer was used (project vs user)
- expected vs actual decision

## Acceptance criteria

- E2E fails if allowlist does not apply in hook mode.
- E2E fails if allowlist is broader than intended.

## Dependencies

- `git_safety_guard-1gt.2.2` evaluator integration
- `git_safety_guard-1gt.2.3` allowlist CLI (optional; direct file edit acceptable in test)
","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-07T22:23:47.275624855-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-07T22:23:47.275624855-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.2.6","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.2","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-07T22:23:58.619659792-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.2.6","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.2.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-07T22:24:03.663391147-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.2.6","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.2.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-07T22:24:08.707714436-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
-{"id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.3","title":"Add decision modes: block vs warn vs log-only","description":"## Objective

Reduce disruption by allowing less-confident detections to warn or log instead of hard-blocking, while keeping high-confidence matches as hard blocks.

## Background

Some detections are extremely high confidence (e.g., `rm -rf /`, `git reset --hard`). Others are context-dependent or susceptible to false positives.

A single binary mode (always deny) can be overly disruptive, especially when expanding into heredoc scanning and AST-based heuristics.

## Proposed behavior

Add a policy layer that can choose between:

- **deny** (current behavior): block command
- **warn**: print warning to stderr and/or log, but allow command (no JSON deny)
- **log-only**: silent allow, but log event for later review

Policy should be configurable:

- global default (e.g., deny)
- per-pack override
- per-pattern override

## Safety constraints

- Default should remain deny for known-catastrophic patterns.
- Warn/log-only must never apply to the most dangerous patterns unless the user explicitly configures it.

## Acceptance criteria

- Config supports selecting mode per pack/pattern.
- Output is correct per mode:
  - deny: stderr warning + stdout JSON deny
  - warn: stderr warning, no stdout
  - log-only: optional log entry, no stderr/stdout
- Tests cover all three modes.

+{"id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.3","title":"Add decision modes: block vs warn vs log-only","description":"## Objective

Reduce disruption by allowing less-confident detections to warn or log instead of hard-blocking, while keeping high-confidence matches as hard blocks.

## Background

Some detections are extremely high confidence (e.g., `rm -rf /`, `git reset --hard`). Others are context-dependent or susceptible to false positives.

A single binary mode (always deny) can be overly disruptive, especially when expanding into heredoc scanning and AST-based heuristics.

## Proposed behavior

Add a policy layer that can choose between:

- **deny** (current behavior): block command
- **warn**: print warning to stderr and/or log, but allow command (no JSON deny)
- **log-only**: silent allow, but log event for later review

Policy should be configurable:

- global default (e.g., deny)
- per-pack override
- per-pattern override

## Safety constraints

- Default should remain deny for known-catastrophic patterns.
- Warn/log-only must never apply to the most dangerous patterns unless the user explicitly configures it.

## Acceptance criteria

- Config supports selecting mode per pack/pattern.
- Output is correct per mode:
  - deny: stderr warning + stdout JSON deny
  - warn: stderr warning, no stdout
  - log-only: optional log entry, no stderr/stdout
- Tests cover all three modes.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.3.1","title":"Decision policy: define rule severity taxonomy + default modes","description":"## Objective

Define an explicit, documented severity taxonomy for rules, and map severity → default decision mode.

This prevents ad-hoc "some things warn, some deny" drift and supports:

- predictable rollout (warn-first where appropriate)
- stable CI policy (`--fail-on error|warning` for scan)
- consistent UX between hook, explain, and scan

## Proposed taxonomy (v1)

- `error` (catastrophic / irreversible): default **DENY**
  - examples: `rm -rf /`, `git reset --hard`, `git clean -fd`, `DROP DATABASE prod`

- `warning` (dangerous but sometimes intentional): default **WARN** (or DENY in strict packs)
  - examples: `git push --force` (could be warn if confidence low)

- `info` (advisory): default **LOG-ONLY**

## Rules of the road

- Severity is a property of a rule (pack_id + pattern_name), not of user configuration.
- User configuration can *tighten* (warn→deny) freely.
- User configuration can *loosen* (deny→warn/log) only with explicit acknowledgement (see allowlist safety rules).

## Acceptance criteria

- `Severity` enum exists and is used consistently across:
  - hook deny/warn/log output
  - `dcg explain`
  - `dcg scan` JSON output
- Default mapping is documented and test-covered.

## Dependencies

- `git_safety_guard-1gt.3` (decision modes implementation)
- `git_safety_guard-99e.2` (stable rule IDs)
","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-07T22:01:58.171989907-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-08T01:50:42.026890924-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-08T01:50:42.026890924-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented Severity and DecisionMode enums with Critical/High/Medium/Low levels. Updated DestructivePattern, DestructiveMatch, CheckResult structs. Added 10 comprehensive tests. All 92 tests pass.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.3.1","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-07T22:02:07.49512402-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.3.1","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-99e.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-07T22:02:19.24160358-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.3.2","title":"Decision policy: tag existing rules with severity + regression tests","description":"## Objective

Apply the severity taxonomy from `git_safety_guard-1gt.3.1` to existing packs and ensure outputs stay stable.

## Scope

- Core packs (`core.git`, `core.filesystem`) first.
- High-signal packs next (docker/k8s/db) as they become reachable in hook mode.

## Requirements

- Each destructive rule has an explicit severity.
- Scan output includes severity for each finding.
- Deny/warn/log mode defaults respect severity mapping.

## Tests

- Snapshot tests for:
  - deny output includes severity
  - explain JSON includes severity
  - scan JSON includes severity
- Regression tests for key rules:
  - `core.git:reset-hard` remains `error`
  - `core.filesystem:rm-rf-non-temp` remains `error`

## Dependencies

- `git_safety_guard-1gt.3.1` (taxonomy)
- `git_safety_guard-99e.2.2` (pattern metadata propagation)

## Acceptance Criteria

- Every rule in core packs has an explicit severity tag.
- Hook output includes severity (or decision mode derived from severity) in a stable, test-covered way.
- `dcg explain` shows severity per match and per final decision.
- `dcg scan --json` emits severity per finding and honors `--fail-on error|warning`.
- Regression tests prevent accidental severity drift for a curated set of high-impact rules.
","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-07T22:02:32.98122712-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-08T13:31:58.561023639-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.3.2","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-07T22:02:42.227156139-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.3.2","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.3.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-07T22:02:54.842192671-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.3.2","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-99e.2.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-07T22:02:59.882469179-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.3.3","title":"Observe mode: warn-first rollout config + expiration","description":"## Objective

Make it easy to roll out DCG in a non-velocity-destroying way by supporting an explicit **observe / warn-first** mode with an optional expiration.

This is a policy/ops feature: it does not change matching, only what DCG *does* when it detects something.

## Requirements

- Config supports a global rollout mode, e.g.:
  - `policy.default_mode = "warn"` (or `log-only`)
  - optional `policy.observe_until = "2026-02-01T00:00:00Z"`

- Semantics:
  - Before `observe_until`: default to WARN for non-catastrophic rules (severity-driven).
  - After `observe_until`: revert to normal defaults (deny for `error`), or emit a clear warning that the observe window expired.

- Safety constraints:
  - Catastrophic rules (severity `error`) should remain DENY unless user explicitly loosens them with an acknowledgement mechanism.

## Acceptance criteria

- Users can enable warn-first rollout without editing per-pack/per-rule settings.
- Behavior is deterministic and test-covered.

## Tests

- Unit tests for:
  - observe window active
  - observe window expired
  - catastrophic rule remains DENY in observe mode

## Dependencies

- `git_safety_guard-1gt.3.1` severity taxonomy
- `git_safety_guard-1gt.3` decision modes infrastructure
","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-07T22:17:33.532547162-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-07T22:17:33.532547162-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.3.3","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-07T22:17:42.627513231-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.3.3","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.3.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-07T22:17:47.669891197-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.3.4","title":"Decision modes: E2E coverage (deny vs warn vs log-only behavior)","description":"## Objective

Add end-to-end tests proving decision modes behave correctly in the hook protocol.

## Why

Decision mode bugs are extremely user-visible:

- WARN must allow (no stdout JSON deny) but still provide stderr output.
- LOG-ONLY must be silent (no stdout, no stderr) but may emit structured logs.

## Test cases

- Configure a non-catastrophic rule to WARN and verify:
  - exit allows
  - stderr contains a warning
  - stdout is empty

- Configure a rule to LOG-ONLY and verify:
  - stdout empty
  - stderr empty

- Verify catastrophic rules remain DENY by default.

## Logging requirements

- Print captured stdout/stderr on failure (truncated).
- Print which config/policy settings were active.

## Acceptance criteria

- Tests would catch accidental protocol regressions (e.g., WARN emitting JSON deny).

## Dependencies

- `git_safety_guard-1gt.3` decision modes implementation
- `git_safety_guard-99e.3.1` shared evaluator
","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-07T22:24:26.009028742-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-07T22:24:26.009028742-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.3.4","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-07T22:24:36.855424505-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.3.4","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-99e.3.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-07T22:24:54.034020082-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.4","title":"Structured logging for allow/deny decisions (optional redaction)","description":"## Objective

Improve operational visibility without compromising privacy by offering structured logs for decisions.

## Background

`dcg` already supports a simple log file for blocked commands. As we add more sophisticated analysis (execution context, heredocs, AST), we need logs that can support:

- diagnosing false positives
- tuning config
- measuring performance impact

## Proposed logging fields

At minimum (on deny/warn/log-only events):

- timestamp
- mode (deny/warn/log-only)
- raw command (optionally redacted)
- normalized command
- pack_id
- pattern_name
- reason
- execution context summary (if available)
- elapsed time (optional)

## Redaction strategy

- Provide a config option to redact arguments beyond a threshold or redact quoted strings.
- Default behavior should be conservative (log only for deny events).

## Acceptance criteria

- Logs are well-formed and append-only.
- A `--json` log format exists (optional).
- Unit tests verify logging output format.
","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-07T20:05:23.438761061-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-07T20:05:23.438761061-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.4","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-07T20:05:23.440080967-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.4","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-99e.3.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-07T20:06:07.298637722-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.4","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-tlog","type":"relates-to","created_at":"2026-01-07T20:11:15.440752673-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.5","title":"Suggestions DB: safe alternatives + how-to-allow guidance","description":"## Objective

Make `dcg` helpful, not just restrictive, by attaching curated suggestions to blocked rules.

When a command is blocked (or warned), the user should immediately see:

- what happened (stable rule id)
- why it's risky
- safe alternatives / preview steps
- how to allow safely (rule-id allowlist command)

## Key idea

Maintain a small, explicit "suggestions database" keyed by stable rule id:

- `rule_id = "pack_id:pattern_name"`

Each rule can have 0..N suggestions:

- `preview_first` (e.g., `git clean -n`, `docker system df`)
- `safer_alternative` (e.g., `--force-with-lease`)
- `workflow_fix` (e.g., `git stash` first)
- `allow_safely` (copy/paste allowlist command)
- `docs` (README anchor)

## Why this is worth doing

- Reduces "rage disable" moments.
- Teaches users safer habits.
- Makes false positives much cheaper to resolve.

## Constraints

- Suggestions must be non-prescriptive when environment-specific.
- Suggestions must be safe-by-default (no destructive commands suggested).
- Hook output must remain bounded in size.

## Acceptance criteria

- For core rules, deny output includes at least one actionable suggestion.
- Explain mode includes suggestions in all formats.
- Suggestions are deterministic and test-covered.
","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-07T21:06:08.978286566-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-07T21:06:08.978286566-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.5","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-07T21:06:18.675450948-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.5.1","title":"Suggestions: data model + registry keyed by rule id","description":"## Objective

Define the suggestion data model and implement a registry lookup by stable rule id.

## Background

When DCG blocks a command, users need actionable guidance:
- What safer alternatives exist?
- How can they preview the effect first?
- How can they allowlist if intentional?

A well-designed suggestion system improves UX and reduces frustration.

## SuggestionKind Enum

```rust
/// Type of suggestion to help the user
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize, Deserialize)]
#[serde(rename_all = "snake_case")]
pub enum SuggestionKind {
    /// "Run this first to preview the effect"
    /// e.g., "Run `git diff` before `git reset --hard`"
    PreviewFirst,

    /// "Use this safer alternative instead"
    /// e.g., "Use `git reset --soft` or `--mixed` instead of `--hard`"
    SaferAlternative,

    /// "Fix your workflow to avoid this situation"
    /// e.g., "Commit your changes before resetting"
    WorkflowFix,

    /// "Read the documentation for more context"
    /// e.g., "See: https://git-scm.com/docs/git-reset"
    Documentation,

    /// "How to allowlist this specific rule"
    /// e.g., "To allow: `dcg allow core.git:reset-hard --reason '...'`"
    AllowSafely,
}
```

## Suggestion Struct

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub struct Suggestion {
    /// Type of suggestion
    pub kind: SuggestionKind,

    /// Human-readable suggestion text
    pub text: String,

    /// Optional command the user can copy/paste
    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
    pub command: Option<String>,

    /// Optional URL for documentation
    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
    pub url: Option<String>,
}
```

## Registry Implementation

```rust
use std::sync::LazyLock;
use std::collections::HashMap;

/// Registry of suggestions keyed by rule_id (e.g., "core.git:reset-hard")
static SUGGESTION_REGISTRY: LazyLock<HashMap<&'static str, Vec<Suggestion>>> =
    LazyLock::new(|| {
        let mut m = HashMap::new();

        m.insert("core.git:reset-hard", vec![
            Suggestion {
                kind: SuggestionKind::PreviewFirst,
                text: "Run `git diff` and `git status` to see what would be lost".into(),
                command: Some("git diff && git status".into()),
                url: None,
            },
            Suggestion {
                kind: SuggestionKind::SaferAlternative,
                text: "Use `git reset --soft` or `--mixed` to preserve changes".into(),
                command: Some("git reset --soft HEAD~1".into()),
                url: None,
            },
            Suggestion {
                kind: SuggestionKind::WorkflowFix,
                text: "Consider using `git stash` to save changes temporarily".into(),
                command: Some("git stash".into()),
                url: None,
            },
        ]);

        // ... more rules
        m
    });

/// Look up suggestions for a rule
pub fn get_suggestions(rule_id: &str) -> Option<&'static [Suggestion]> {
    SUGGESTION_REGISTRY.get(rule_id).map(|v| v.as_slice())
}
```

## Output Formatting

### Hook Deny Output (stderr)
Show only top 1-3 suggestions to keep output bounded:

```
╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║  ⚠️  BLOCKED: git reset --hard HEAD~5                        ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║  Rule: core.git:reset-hard                                   ║
║  Reason: Hard reset can permanently lose uncommitted changes ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║  💡 Try instead:                                             ║
║  • Run `git diff` first to see what would be lost            ║
║  • Use `git reset --soft` to preserve changes                ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
```

### Explain Mode (JSON)
Include all suggestions in structured format:

```json
{
  "decision": "deny",
  "rule_id": "core.git:reset-hard",
  "reason": "Hard reset can permanently lose uncommitted changes",
  "suggestions": [
    {
      "kind": "preview_first",
      "text": "Run `git diff` and `git status` to see what would be lost",
      "command": "git diff && git status"
    },
    {
      "kind": "safer_alternative",
      "text": "Use `git reset --soft` or `--mixed` to preserve changes",
      "command": "git reset --soft HEAD~1"
    }
  ]
}
```

## Requirements

- Registry lookup is O(1) via HashMap
- Zero allocations on lookup (return static references)
- Suggestions are stable and deterministic (iteration order preserved)
- JSON output uses snake_case for kind values

## Acceptance Criteria

- Registry lookup is fast and allocation-light.
- JSON output for explain includes suggestions in a stable order.
- All SuggestionKind variants are used appropriately across rules.

## Tests

- Unit tests for registry lookup and ordering.
- Snapshot tests for JSON output format.

Core git/filesystem rules populated.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.5.1","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.5","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-07T21:07:01.11809851-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.5.1","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-99e.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-07T21:22:31.439334729-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
-{"id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.5.2","title":"Suggestions: populate core.git + core.filesystem","description":"## Objective

Populate suggestions for the highest-traffic and highest-impact rules:

- `core.git:*`
- `core.filesystem:*`

## What to include

For each rule:

- a short "preview first" command when applicable
- a safer alternative when one exists
- the recommended allowlist command (rule-id based)

Examples (conceptual):

- `core.git:reset-hard`:
  - suggest `git stash` / `git diff` / `git reset --soft|--mixed`
- `core.git:push-force-*`:
  - suggest `--force-with-lease`
- `core.filesystem:rm-rf-*`:
  - suggest verifying path and using safer deletion flows when possible

## Acceptance criteria

- Every core rule has at least one suggestion.
- Suggestions are accurate and generic (no environment assumptions).

## Tests

- A "coverage" test that asserts all core destructive patterns have at least 1 suggestion.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.5.3","title":"Suggestions: populate high-signal non-core packs (docker/k8s/db)","description":"## Objective

Populate suggestions for the packs most likely to appear in real dev workflows:

- Docker
- kubectl
- databases (psql/mysql/redis)

## Guidance

Prefer "preview" suggestions:

- docker: `docker system df` before `docker system prune`
- kubectl: `--dry-run=client` / `kubectl diff`
- SQL: backup / transaction guidance (keep generic)

## Acceptance criteria

- High-signal destructive patterns in these packs have suggestions.
- Suggestions are concise and safe.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.5.4","title":"Integrate suggestions into deny output and explain mode","description":"## Objective

Make suggestions visible where they matter:

- hook deny output (stderr) includes the top suggestion(s)
- `dcg explain` includes all suggestions (pretty/json/compact)

This task is also where we implement the **guided learning workflow** (hook-safe, non-interactive):

- on block, print copy/pasteable next steps (`dcg explain ...`, `dcg allowlist add ...`)
- no prompts; the hook must never wait for user input

## Requirements

### Hook deny output

- Must remain bounded; show only top 1–3 suggestions.
- Must include:
  - stable rule id (`pack_id:pattern_name`)
  - a short reason
  - at least one safe alternative suggestion (when available)
  - a copy/paste allowlist command example, e.g.:
    - `dcg allowlist add <rule_id> --reason "..." --project`
  - a copy/paste explain command:
    - `dcg explain "<command>"`

### Explain output

- Show all suggestions.
- JSON includes suggestions as structured objects.

## Acceptance criteria

- Deny output shows stable rule id + at least one suggestion.
- Deny output includes copy/paste learning commands (explain + allowlist add).
- Explain mode shows suggestions in all formats.

## Tests

- Snapshot tests for deny output (stderr) for a few core rules.
- Explain output tests include suggestions list.
- Regression test: deny output stays under a size cap even if a rule has many suggestions.
","notes":"## Review Additions (2026-01-08)

### Determinism
- Suggestion ordering must be deterministic:
  - sort by severity then stable rule ID
  - cap list with a stable tie-breaker
- Output formatting must not depend on hash iteration order.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-07T21:06:46.881705224-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-07T23:26:16.266936339-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.5.4","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.5","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-07T21:07:16.392198141-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.5.4","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.5.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-07T21:22:46.724344957-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.5.4","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.1.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-07T21:22:51.819635023-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.5.4","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.1.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-07T21:22:56.914257712-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.5.4","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.2.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-07T21:23:02.00798218-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.5.5","title":"Suggestions: correctness and coverage tests","description":"## Objective

Prevent suggestion drift and ensure suggestions remain helpful.

## Tests

- Coverage:
  - assert that all patterns with `pattern_name` in core packs have at least one suggestion
- Correctness (spot checks):
  - ensure suggestions never include explicitly forbidden destructive commands (e.g., do not suggest `rm -rf`)
- Ordering:
  - stable output ordering across runs

## Acceptance criteria

- Suggestions are maintained as a first-class UX surface and do not regress silently.
